env.globals["strftime_now"] = lambda fmt: "2026-07-21"

from llm_workflow_agents.training.grpo import _load_grpo_jsonl

input.content:
# Cat A "Announce-but-Don't-Call" Forensics — Results & Next Step

## Context

Phase 2 Cat A (Gemma-4-26B-A4B, SFT ckpt-1000) is under the 0.75 held-out target
(latest re-measurement: 0.7167 FAIL, commit `d53e3b6`) with no cheap RL lever
(RFT MARGINAL, GRPO dead). The residual gap was attributed to one "genuine weakness":
**announce-but-don't-call** — on tool-expected held-out turns the model narrates the
correct state transition + intent but under-emits the `<tool_call>` tag.

Before spending GPU on any SFT retrain or RFT pilot, `docs/grpo_tool_emission_gap_review.md`
§4 step 1 (Fable-reviewed) mandates three cheap forensics checks to rule out non-policy
explanations. The user asked to run them. **This plan records what the checks found** — it
is a findings + decision doc, not a code change. The only outstanding implementation is the
one GPU-gated check.

## What was run (read-only, this session)

All against the canonical, post-R12-fix `data/output/grpo/task_a`, using the **real
production slicer** `_load_grpo_jsonl` (`src/llm_workflow_agents/training/grpo.py:236`) and
ckpt-1000's **actual** `chat_template.jinja` — not a reconstruction.

### Check 1 — Row-slicing exposure bias → **CONFIRMED (reframes the weakness)**
Sliced the 290-conv validation split into 2,943 single-turn GRPO rows; 1,126 are
tool-expected (GT carries `tool_calls`). Classified each target turn:

| Target shape | Count | % of tool-expected |
|---|---|---|
| **Bare call** (tool_call only, no narration in the target turn) | 794 | **70.5%** |
| Fused (narration + tool_call in the same turn) | 332 | 29.5% |

Of the 794 bare-call targets, **100% are preceded by an assistant turn that narrates the
intent without emitting a tool call**. So the gold corpus genuinely encodes the
narrate-then-call rhythm as **two separate assistant turns** the majority of the time
(pattern: `assistant narrates & asks → user replies → assistant emits bare call`).

**Implication:** Fable's hypothesis holds. The corpus trains *both* "narrate without

Ran the three cheap, no-training checks §4 step 1 called for, against the canonical
post-R12-fix `data/output/grpo/task_a` (290-conv validation), using the actual production
slicer (`_load_grpo_jsonl`, `training/grpo.py:236`) and ckpt-1000's real `chat_template.jinja`
— not reconstructions. 2/3 completed; the third needs the GPU host.

### 7.1 Row-slicing exposure bias — CONFIRMED, reframes the "genuine weakness" claim

Sliced validation into 2,943 single-turn rows; 1,126 are tool-expected (GT carries
`tool_calls`). Classified each target turn's shape:

| Target shape | Count | % of tool-expected |
|---|---|---|
| Bare call (tool_call only, no narration in that turn) | 794 | **70.5%** |
| Fused (narration + tool_call in the same turn) | 332 | 29.5% |

Of the 794 bare-call targets, **100%** are immediately preceded (across a user turn) by an
assistant turn that narrates intent *without* calling. So the teacher corpus itself encodes
narrate-then-call as **two separate assistant turns** the majority of the time — pattern:
`assistant narrates & asks a clarifying/confirming question → user replies → assistant fires
a bare tool call`.

**This confirms the row-slicing hypothesis from §3.** The gold corpus trains *both*
"narrate without calling" (the announce turn — a legitimately zero-tool gold target on its
own row) and "call without narrating" (the bare-call turn) as equally correct, turn-local
behaviors. Combined with the 61.1% zero-tool row imbalance (§2 of the companion doc), the
policy has strong incentive to reproduce the announce-style turn, and on a single-turn
teacher-forced held-out eval it sometimes does so exactly where GT expects the bare call
instead. **A material share of the measured "announce-but-don't-call" gap is therefore a
data-structure + single-turn-eval-granularity artifact, not a pure policy defect** — this is
the exact confound the Fable review (§3) warned would make an uncontrolled retrain
unattributable. It does not mean the weakness is fake; it means the *single-turn* tool-F1
number likely understates true tool-emission competence, and a free-running multi-turn probe
is needed before sizing any fix.

### 7.2 Chat-template system-prompt folding — CLEAN, no bug found

Rendered a real sliced GRPO validation prompt (17-message multi-turn history, enriched
system prompt with workflow script + tool descriptions) through ckpt-1000's actual
`chat_template.jinja` via a minimal sandboxed Jinja environment (mirrors HF's
`apply_chat_template` semantics: `trim_blocks`/`lstrip_blocks`, `raise_exception`,
`strftime_now` globals). Renders cleanly: the system message folds into a single
`<|turn>system … <turn|>` block, no `TemplateError`, no dropped content, and
`add_generation_prompt` opens a clean `<|turn>model` slot for the completion. Rules out
silent schema/system-prompt loss as a contributor. Note: `03-training.md`'s "Gemma rejects
bare `system` role" caveat does not apply to this checkpoint's template — Task A carries tool
info as system-prompt text (not the `tools=` kwarg), and this template explicitly special-cases
`messages[0]['role'] in ['system', 'developer']` (`chat_template.jinja:179-195`).

### 7.3 finish_reason / truncation — BLOCKED, needs the GPU host

Requires either live generation metadata for the 15 announce-but-don't-call held-out rows,
or the artifact `runs/preflight/heldout_composite_audit_ckpt1000.json`. Neither exists in a
no-CUDA session — `runs/preflight/` here holds only a provenance txt, no completions.
**Unresolved**, carries forward as the one remaining §4-step-1 item.

**Revised next step:** given §7.1, don't read the single-turn tool-F1 gap as a clean policy
ceiling. On the next GPU-host session, pair the blocked §7.3 finish_reason check with a
**free-running multi-turn probe**: let the model continue past an announce-style turn and
check whether it fires the bare call on its own next turn once a user reply arrives. If it
does so reliably, the true deployed-eval tool-emission rate is materially higher than 0.087
tool-F1 and the priority shifts from "fix the policy" to "fix the single-turn eval + re-derive
the 0.75 bar" (§6.1's outstanding item) rather than an SFT retrain or RFT pilot.

content.text:
Ran the three §4-step-1 forensics checks (no GPU needed for two of them, as I'd said). Results:

**Check 1 — row-slicing exposure bias: CONFIRMED, and it reframes the diagnosis.** Sliced the real 290-conversation validation set with the actual production loader (`_load_grpo_jsonl`). Of 1,126 tool-expected rows, 70.5% are "bare call" targets (a tool call with no narration) — and **100% of those** are immediately preceded by an assistant turn that narrates the same intent *without* calling, separated by a user turn. The teacher corpus itself splits "announce" and "call" into two separate assistant turns most of the time, and single-turn slicing trains both halves as independently correct. So a real chunk of the measured announce-but-don't-call gap is a data-structure/eval-granularity artifact, not a pure policy defect — the 0.087 single-turn tool-F1 likely understates true tool-emission competence.

**Check 3 — chat-template folding: CLEAN.** Rendered a real prompt through ckpt-1000's actual `chat_template.jinja` — the system prompt (workflow script + tool descriptions) folds in correctly, no errors, nothing dropped. The "Gemma rejects bare system role" caveat in `03-training.md` turns out not to apply to this checkpoint's custom template.

**Check 2 — finish_reason/truncation: still blocked.** Needs GPU-host generation artifacts that don't exist in this no-CUDA session.

I recorded the findings in `docs/grpo_tool_emission_gap_review.md` §7 (matching the doc's existing format) and updated my project memory. The recommended next step, given check 1's result: on the next GPU-host session, run the blocked finish_reason check alongside a **free-running multi-turn probe** — let the model continue past an announce-style turn and see if it fires the call on its own next turn once a user reply lands, mirroring how the corpus is actually structured. That determines whether this is mostly an eval artifact (in which case the fix is re-deriving the target bar, not retraining) before committing to any SFT/RFT work.

No code or data changed — this was read-only investigation plus a docs update. Nothing committed to git yet.
